steps.title: Recognize the mid-sentence appositive
steps.content: The phrase "a lawyer and cycling advocate" is an appositive—a noun phrase that renames "Ernesto Hernandez-Lopez" and appears between the subject and the main verb "has identified".

steps.title: Apply the nonrestrictive appositive rule
steps.content:
Rule: a nonrestrictive appositive (extra, non-identifying information) that interrupts the main clause must be enclosed in commas on both sides. "a lawyer and cycling advocate" adds description about Hernandez-Lopez but doesn't identify which Hernandez-Lopez — it's nonrestrictive and needs two commas.

A: "Ernesto Hernandez-Lopez a lawyer and cycling advocate has identified" — no commas at all around the appositive. Incorrect.

B: "Ernesto Hernandez-Lopez, a lawyer and cycling advocate, has identified" — commas on both sides properly enclose the nonrestrictive appositive. Correct.

C: "Ernesto Hernandez-Lopez a lawyer and cycling advocate, has identified" — only a trailing comma; the opening comma is missing. Incorrect.

D: "Ernesto Hernandez-Lopez, a lawyer and cycling advocate has identified" — only a leading comma; the closing comma is missing, leaving the appositive unclosed. Incorrect.
